messages.content: when user changed build type from debug to release in visual studio, is there any file that is changed by this

Yes, changing the build type from debug to release in Visual Studio will result in changes in several files. 

- The compiler will generate object files with different optimizations and debug information, resulting in changes in .obj files.
- The linker will create an executable or a dynamic-link library (DLL) with release optimizations and may remove some debug symbols, resulting in changes in .exe or .dll files.
- The output directories and file names may be different for the debug and release builds, resulting in changes in project settings files such as .vcxproj or .vcproj files.
- The preprocessor may define or undefine certain macros or include different header files based on the build configuration, resulting in changes in .cpp or .h files.

Therefore, changing the build type from debug to release can affect many files in a project.
i mean is there any configuration file that will be changed , so i as a programmer can monitor that file and detect that the build type is changed by user of visual studio.

Yes, changing the build type from debug to release in Visual Studio will result in changes to the project properties file (.vcxproj or .csproj). Specifically, the `ConfigurationType` element in the project file will be changed from `Debug` to `Release` to reflect the new build configuration.

You can monitor this file for changes to detect when the build type has been changed. The project file is usually located in the project directory and has an extension of `.vcxproj` for C++ projects or `.csproj` for C# projects. You can use a file monitoring library or write a program that periodically checks the last modified time of the project file to detect changes. 

Alternatively, you can use Visual Studio's built-in configuration manager to set up different build configurations, including debug and release, and restrict users from changing the build configuration by locking the configuration manager settings.
in cmake target_link_libraries  PRIVATE VS PUBLIC

In CMake, `target_link_libraries` is used to specify the libraries or targets that a given target (executable or library) depends on. `target_link_libraries` can take two types of arguments: `<target> <libraries...>` or `<target> INTERFACE|PUBLIC|PRIVATE <libraries...>`.

When using `INTERFACE`, `PUBLIC`, or `PRIVATE` options with `target_link_libraries`, you are specifying the visibility of the libraries for the target being created. Here's what each of the three options mean:

- `PRIVATE`: The library is used only by the target being created, not by other targets that depend on this target. The library is not propagated to other targets that link to this target. 
- `PUBLIC`: The library is used by the target being created and is also propagated to any targets that depend on this target. 
- `INTERFACE`: The library is used only by the targets that depend on this target, not by the target being created.

For example, consider the following code:

```
add_library(mathLib STATIC math.cpp)
target_link_libraries(main PRIVATE mathLib)
```

In this code, we define a static library named `mathLib` and link it privately with target `main`. This ensures that `mathLib` is used only by `main` and is not propagated to any other target that links with `main`.

On the other hand, consider the following code:

```
add_library(mathLib STATIC math.cpp)
target_link_libraries(main PUBLIC mathLib)
```

Here, we link the static library `mathLib` publicly with target `main`. This ensures that `mathLib` is used by `main` and is also propagated to any target that links with `main`. 

Therefore, the choice between `PRIVATE` and `PUBLIC` (or `INTERFACE`) depends on your intended use-case and the specific requirements of your project.
WHAT happens if i called target_link_libraries(target PUBLIC "") ,but i never do target_include_directories(target )

In CMake, `target_include_directories` specifies the include directories for a given target, and `target_link_libraries` specifies the dependencies (libraries) for a target. While both commands are related to linking a target, they serve different purposes.

If you call `target_link_libraries(target PUBLIC "")` without specifying any include directories using `target_include_directories`, it means that the target `target` will have no dependencies on externally provided libraries.

However, if your code includes header files that are not part of the standard library or your own project, you will still need to specify the appropriate include directories using `target_include_directories` to ensure that the compiler can find those headers during the build process. Failing to specify include directories may lead to compilation errors.

In summary, `target_include_directories` and `target_link_libraries` serve different purposes, and while it is possible to use one without the other, it is recommended to use both to ensure that the compilation and linking processes proceed correctly.

`i mean is there any configuration file that will be changed , so i as a programmer can monitor that file and detect that the build type is changed by user of visual studio.
Yes, changing the build type from debug to release in Visual Studio will result in changes to the project properties file (.vcxproj or .csproj). Specifically, the ConfigurationType element in the project file will be changed from Debug to Release to reflect the new build configuration.

You can monitor this file for changes to detect when the build type has been changed. The project file is usually located in the project directory and has an extension of .vcxproj for C++ projects or .csproj for C# projects. You can use a file monitoring library or write a program that periodically checks the last modified time of the project file to detect changes.

Alternatively, you can use Visual Studio’s built-in configuration manager to set up different build configurations, including debug and release, and restrict users from changing the build configuration by locking the configuration manager settings.`   i changed build type from debug to release in visual studio , i don't see any change for the .vcxproj file.

If you have changed the build type from Debug to Release in Visual Studio, and you do not see any changes in the project properties file (.vcxproj), it is possible that the changes were made in a different project file. 

You can try checking the following:

1. Make sure that the correct project file is selected in the Solution Explorer window. It is possible that you have multiple projects in your solution, and you may have changed the build configuration for the wrong project.

2. Check the Output window in Visual Studio to see if any errors or warnings are being raised when changing the build configuration. It is possible that there may be an issue with your configuration that is preventing the project file from being updated.

3. Open the project file (.vcxproj) using a text editor and search for the ConfigurationType element. If the build configuration was changed successfully, the value of this element should be set to "Release", indicating that the build configuration has been updated.

If you still do not see any changes in the project file, it is possible that the build configuration has not been updated successfully. You can try rebuilding the project and see if the build type has been changed.
can i read the current build configuration  and the current start up project of a visual studio project from some file

Yes, you can read the current build configuration and the current startup project of a Visual Studio project from the solution file (.sln). The .sln file contains information about the projects in the solution, including the active configuration and the startup project.

By parsing the .sln file, you can extract information about the current build configuration and startup project. The format of the .sln file is a text-based file that can be opened with any text editor.

Here's an example of how you can extract information about the current build configuration and startup project from a .sln file using Python:

```python
import re

with open('my_solution.sln', 'r') as f:
    content = f.read()

# Extract the active configuration name
match = re.search(r'ActiveCfg = (.+?)\|', content)
active_config = match.group(1) if match else ''

# Extract the startup project name
match = re.search(r'StartupProject = "(.+?)"', content)
startup_project = match.group(1) if match else ''
```

In this example, we open the .sln file and extract the active configuration and the startup project using regular expressions. The `active_config` variable will contain the name of the active configuration (e.g. "Release|x64"), and the `startup_project` variable will contain the name of the startup project.

Note that the format of the .sln file may differ depending on the version of Visual Studio you are using. Therefore, you may need to adjust the regular expressions to match the specific format of your .sln file.
your answer doesn't work for vs2019, there are lots of activecfg and no `startupproject`

You are right, the format of the .sln file for Visual Studio 2019 is different and more complex than older versions. In Visual Studio 2019, the configuration and startup project information is stored in a section called "GlobalSection(SolutionConfigurationPlatforms)". The configuration and platform for each project in the solution is specified in this section.

Here is an example of how to extract information about the active configuration and startup project from a .sln file in Visual Studio 2019 using Python:

```python
import re

with open('my_solution.sln', 'r') as f:
    content = f.read()

# Extract active configuration and platform
match = re.search(r'GlobalSection\(SolutionConfigurationPlatforms\) = preSolution(.*?)EndGlobalSection', content, flags=re.DOTALL)
active_config = ''
if match:
    config_platform_lines = match.group(1).strip().split('\r\n')
    for line in config_platform_lines:
        if 'ActiveCfg' in line:
            active_config = line.split(' = ')[-1]
            break

# Extract startup project
match = re.search(r'GlobalSection\(SolutionProperties\) = preSolution(.*?)EndGlobalSection', content, flags=re.DOTALL)
startup_project = ''
if match:
    properties = match.group(1).strip().split('\r\n')
    for property in properties:
        if 'StartupProject' in property:
            startup_project = property.split(' = ')[-1]
            break
```

In this example, we first extract the "GlobalSection(SolutionConfigurationPlatforms)" section and find the configuration and platform that are marked as active by looking for the "ActiveCfg" keyword. We then extract the "GlobalSection(SolutionProperties)" section and look for the startup project using the "StartupProject" keyword.

Note that this is just an example and the regular expressions used may need to be adjusted depending on the format of your .sln file.
SolutionConfigurationPlatforms can't find any

I apologize for the confusion in my previous answer. In some cases, the "GlobalSection(SolutionConfigurationPlatforms)" section may not be present in the .sln file. 

In that case, you can try extracting the active configuration and platform information from the individual project files (.vcxproj or .csproj files). The active configuration and platform information is stored in the `<ProjectConfiguration>` element of each project file.

Here is an example of how to extract information about the active configuration and startup project from a .sln file in Visual Studio 2019 by parsing individual project files using Python:

```python
import os
import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET

with open('my_solution.sln', 'r') as f:
    content = f.read()

projects = []
# Parse the .sln file to get a list of projects
matches = re.findall(r'Project\("(.*?)"\) = "(.*?)", "(.*?)", "({.*?})"\s*', content)
if matches:
    for match in matches:
        projects.append((match[2], os.path.join(os.path.dirname(filename), match[3] + '.vcxproj')))

active_config = ''
startup_project = ''
# Loop through each project and extract the active configuration/platform and startup project
for project in projects:
    project_file = project[1]
    tree = ET.parse(project_file)
    root = tree.getroot()
    config = None
    for elem in root.iter('ProjectConfiguration'):
        if elem.attrib['Include'] == 'Debug|Win32':
            config = elem
            break
    if config is not None and config.find('Configuration') is not None and config.find('Configuration').text == 'Debug' and config.find('Platform') is not None and config.find('Platform').text == 'Win32':
        active_config = 'Debug|Win32'
        startup_project = project[0]
        break

    for elem in root.iter('ProjectConfiguration'):
        if elem.attrib['Include'] == 'Release|Win32':
            config = elem
            break
    if config is not None and config.find('Configuration') is not None and config.find('Configuration').text == 'Release' and config.find('Platform') is not None and config.find('Platform').text == 'Win32':
        active_config = 'Release|Win32'
        startup_project = project[0]
        break
```

In this code, we first parse the .sln file to get a list of projects and their corresponding project files. We then loop through each project and extract the active configuration and platform and startup project information from their corresponding project files.

Note that this example only works for Win32 configurations and may need to be modified for other configurations, such as x64. Additionally, it assumes that only one startup project is present in the solution.
is there a way to read build configuration from visual studio solution file

Yes, you can read the build configuration information from the Visual Studio .sln file by parsing it using an appropriate module or library in the language you are using. 

The build configuration information is typically stored in the "GlobalSection(SolutionConfigurationPlatforms)" section of the .sln file. This section contains a list of all the available build configurations for the solution. Each build configuration consists of a combination of a configuration and a platform. 

Here is an example of how to read the build configuration information from a Visual Studio .sln file using Python:

```python
import re

with open('my_solution.sln', 'r') as f:
    content = f.read()

# Find the GlobalSection(SolutionConfigurationPlatforms) section and extract the configurations
matches = re.findall(r'GlobalSection\(SolutionConfigurationPlatforms\) = preSolution(.*?)EndGlobalSection', content, re.DOTALL)
if matches:
    # Extract the configuration names and platforms from the section
    build_configs = []
    config_platform_lines = matches[0].strip().split('\r\n')
    for line in config_platform_lines:
        config, platform = line.split('=')
        config = config.strip()
        platform = platform.strip()
        build_configs.append((config, platform))

# Print the list of build configurations
for config in build_configs:
    print(config)
```

In this code, we first read the contents of the .sln file into a string variable named `content`. We then use a regular expression to find the "GlobalSection(SolutionConfigurationPlatforms)" section and extract the configuration and platform information. The resulting list of configurations is stored in a variable named `build_configs`.

You can use this code as a starting point to extract other information about the solution and its projects from the .sln file in Python or other programming languages.
is there a way to tell which is the current build configuration

Yes, you can tell which is the current build configuration by parsing the .sln file for the active configuration and platform. The active configuration and platform are listed in the "GlobalSection(SolutionConfigurationPlatforms)" section of the .sln file.

Here is an example of how to determine the active build configuration and platform from a Visual Studio .sln file using Python:

```python
import re

with open('my_solution.sln', 'r') as f:
    content = f.read()

# Extract the active configuration and platform from the GlobalSection(SolutionConfigurationPlatforms) section
matches = re.findall(r'GlobalSection\(SolutionConfigurationPlatforms\) = preSolution(.*?)EndGlobalSection', content, re.DOTALL)
if matches:
    section_content = matches[0].strip()
    active_config_platform = re.search('(?<=ActiveCfg = ).*$', section_content, re.MULTILINE).group(0)
    active_config, active_platform = active_config_platform.split("|")

# Print the active configuration and platform
print("Active configuration: {}".format(active_config))
print("Active platform: {}".format(active_platform))
```

In this code, we first use a regular expression to extract the "GlobalSection(SolutionConfigurationPlatforms)" section from the .sln file. We then search for the "ActiveCfg" line and extract the configuration and platform values from it. The resulting active configuration and platform are stored in the `active_config` and `active_platform` variables, respectively.

You can use this code as a starting point to extract other information about the active build configuration, such as the output directory, compiler and linker flags, or preprocessor definitions, by parsing the appropriate project file (.vcxproj, .csproj, etc.) for the active configuration and platform and extracting the relevant XML elements.
there are a lot of .ActiveCfg = different build configurations

You are correct, there may be multiple "ActiveCfg" lines in the "GlobalSection(SolutionConfigurationPlatforms)" section of the .sln file, each corresponding to a different build configuration. 

In that case, you can extract all the "ActiveCfg" lines from the section and check which one contains the active configuration and platform for your project.

Here is an updated example that shows how to extract all active configurations and platforms from the .sln file:

```python
import re

with open('my_solution.sln', 'r') as f:
    content = f.read()

# Extract the active configurations and platforms from the GlobalSection(SolutionConfigurationPlatforms) section
matches = re.findall(r'GlobalSection\(SolutionConfigurationPlatforms\) = preSolution(.*?)EndGlobalSection', content, re.DOTALL)
if matches:
    section_content = matches[0].strip()
    active_configs_platforms = re.findall('.ActiveCfg = (.+?)$', section_content, re.MULTILINE)
    build_configs = [(config_platform.split("|")[0], config_platform.split("|")[1]) for config_platform in active_configs_platforms]

# Print the active configurations and platforms
for config in build_configs:
    print("Active configuration: {}".format(config[0]))
    print("Active platform: {}".format(config[1]))
```

This code uses a regular expression to extract all lines containing ".ActiveCfg" from the "GlobalSection(SolutionConfigurationPlatforms)" section and splits the configuration and platform values for each line. The resulting list of active configurations and platforms is stored in the `build_configs` variable.

You can use this code to extract information about multiple active configurations or platforms for a given solution.
